name: GHK-Cu (Copper Peptide)
summary: GHK-Cu is a naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide that declines with age and is widely used to support skin remodeling, collagen synthesis, and wound healing. It is best known as a cosmetic anti-aging agent but also has regenerative and tissue-repair properties.
mechanism:
GHK (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine) is a tripeptide present in human plasma, saliva, and urine that has a high affinity for copper(II), forming the GHK-Cu complex. Plasma levels are roughly 200 ng/mL at age 20 and fall to about 80 ng/mL by age 60, paralleling the age-related decline in regenerative capacity. The complex acts as a copper carrier and signaling molecule that stimulates dermal fibroblasts to increase synthesis of collagen, elastin, glycosaminoglycans, and proteoglycans such as decorin, while remodeling the extracellular matrix.

GHK-Cu modulates matrix metalloproteinases and their inhibitors (TIMPs) to clear damaged ECM proteins, promotes angiogenesis and nerve outgrowth, and exerts antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Gene-expression studies show it can reset thousands of genes toward a younger, healthier state, downregulating pro-fibrotic TGF-beta signaling to favor scar-free healing and upregulating tissue-repair pathways. These actions underlie its use in skin rejuvenation, hair follicle stimulation, and wound repair.
benefits: Stimulates collagen, elastin, and glycosaminoglycan synthesis for firmer, smoother skin
Reduces appearance of fine lines, wrinkles, and skin sagging
Accelerates wound closure (reported ~40-50% faster in models) with reduced scarring
Improves skin elasticity, texture, and pigmentation
Promotes angiogenesis and supports hair follicle health/regrowth
Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory protection against environmental damage
risks: Contraindicated in active malignancy; copper-driven angiogenesis could theoretically support tumor growth
Injectable use is not FDA-approved and lacks human clinical safety data
Excess copper exposure risk with high systemic dosing; avoid in copper metabolism disorders (e.g. Wilson's disease)
sideEffects: Mild injection-site redness, irritation, or stinging
Temporary skin irritation or contact dermatitis with topical use
Transient headache or lightheadedness with systemic dosing

dosage.notes: Topical: 1-2% serum concentration applied daily (no cycling needed). Injectable (subcutaneous) educational protocols start at 1 mg/day, titrating to 2-3 mg/day after ~2 weeks if well tolerated.
dosage.timing: Topical: apply to clean, dry skin once or twice daily (AM and/or PM). Separate application from high-strength vitamin C or acidic actives, which can destabilize the copper complex. Cosmetic formulations are commonly 0.05-3%; clinical skin trials use daily application for ~12 weeks.
dosage.maxDose: Topical formulations generally stay at or below ~3% GHK-Cu; higher concentrations raise irritation/staining risk without clear added benefit. For anecdotal injectable use, roughly 3 mg/day systemic is a commonly cited self-imposed ceiling to limit copper load - this is not an established safe limit.

steps.note: Representative per-application amount delivered by a ~1-2% serum (roughly 1-2 mg GHK-Cu per ~0.1 mL pump). Apply daily; continuous use, no cycling. Clinical skin studies typically run 12 weeks of daily application.

protocols.label: Anecdotal SUBQ (not route-of-record; injectable use is not FDA-approved)

steps.note: Starting dose to assess tolerance; injectable human protocols are anecdotal and lack clinical safety data.
Titrate up if well tolerated; commonly a 30-day active block, then break.

cycleLength: Topical: continuous, no cycling. Injectable: typically 4-8 weeks on, often a 30-day active cycle with optional breaks.

reconstitution.notes: 50 mg vial + 5 mL bacteriostatic water = 10 mg/mL, so 2 mg = 0.2 mL (20 units on a U-100 insulin syringe). Alternatively 50 mg + 2 mL = 25 mg/mL. The blue-green color is normal and indicates the intact copper complex.

storage: Store lyophilized vials frozen at -20C. After reconstitution, refrigerate at 2-8C and use within ~30 days. Protect from light.
contraindications: Active cancer or history of malignancy
Wilson's disease or other copper metabolism disorders
Known hypersensitivity to copper or the peptide

interactions.note: High-strength vitamin C can destabilize the copper complex if layered together topically; apply at separate times.
Often combined for wound/skin repair; complementary regenerative and angiogenic effects.

references.title: Regenerative and Protective Actions of the GHK-Cu Peptide in the Light of the New Gene Data (PMC)
Skin Regenerative and Anti-Cancer Actions of Copper Peptides (Cosmetics, MDPI)
Copper Peptide (GHK-Cu): Clinical Uses, Stability & Compounding (Fagron Academy)
GHK-Cu Peptide: Skin & Healing Benefits (Paragon Sports Medicine)
Epigenetic mechanisms activated by GHK-Cu increase skin collagen density in clinical trial (EurekAlert!)
